title: Keeper vs Buttercup for Busy professionals
meta_description: Keeper vs Buttercup for busy professionals sharing team credentials. See why Keeper wins when permission based vault sharing is required.
constraint_lens: Busy professionals prefer tools that let teams share credentials instantly without passing vault files around.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for busy professionals who need to share credentials with teammates using built in permission controls.
one_second_verdict.reason: Buttercup fails first because sharing requires manually distributing encrypted vault files instead of assigning permissions in a shared vault.

verdict.summary: Keeper is the better option for busy professionals who frequently share credentials with coworkers. It provides a shared vault workspace where administrators can grant permission to specific logins. Buttercup stores credentials inside encrypted vault files that must be shared manually with teammates. For teams that need quick collaboration, passing vault files creates delays and confusion.
verdict.decision_rule: If sharing credentials requires manually distributing vault files instead of assigning permissions inside a shared vault workspace, Buttercup fails first.

sections.heading: Why Keeper fits busy professionals
sections.content: The user shares credentials with coworkers and needs controlled access to each login. Keeper provides a shared vault where administrators can assign access permissions to specific credentials. Team members log into their accounts and see only the passwords they are allowed to access. This allows teams to collaborate without manually distributing encrypted vault files.

bullets.point: Keeper allows administrators to assign access permissions to individual credentials inside a shared vault.
bullets.why_it_matters: Teammates can open shared passwords instantly without exchanging vault files.

bullets.point: Keeper updates shared credentials centrally inside the hosted vault workspace.
bullets.why_it_matters: When a password changes, every authorized teammate sees the new version automatically.

bullets.point: Keeper organizes credentials inside team vault folders with controlled access.
bullets.why_it_matters: Large sets of credentials can be shared across departments without exposing everything to everyone.

bullets.point: Buttercup stores passwords inside a local encrypted vault file.
bullets.why_it_matters: Users maintain full control over where the vault file is stored.

bullets.point: Buttercup works completely offline once the vault file exists.
bullets.why_it_matters: Passwords remain accessible even without internet connectivity.

bullets.point: Buttercup allows vault files to be stored in user controlled storage locations.
bullets.why_it_matters: Advanced users can manage backups and storage independently.

items.fails_when: The user refuses to store credentials inside a hosted vault service.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Buttercup where passwords are stored inside a local encrypted vault file.

items.fails_when: Multiple teammates must access shared credentials because Buttercup requires manually distributing encrypted vault files.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Keeper where credential access can be assigned inside a shared vault workspace.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: If the user manages passwords alone and prefers storing the vault locally instead of inside a hosted service, Buttercup may become the better option.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Pick Keeper if your team needs permission based access to shared credentials.
Pick Keeper if coworkers must see updated passwords automatically.
Pick Buttercup if you want to keep the password vault entirely local.

faqs.a: Keeper provides shared vault workspaces where administrators can assign credential access permissions.

faqs.q: Can Buttercup share credentials with teams easily?
faqs.a: No. Buttercup stores passwords in encrypted vault files that must be distributed manually.

faqs.q: Does Keeper update shared passwords automatically?
faqs.a: Yes. When a password changes in the shared vault, all authorized teammates see the updated version.
Users who want to keep passwords inside a local encrypted vault file may prefer Buttercup.
